coredata.dc:description: Most reported solid-state fluorescence materials utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) rely on simply physical dispersing in polymer or gel matrices to suppress self-quenching, resulting in the issue of fluorescence instability. Here, we initially utilize crop waste peanut shells and add ethylenediamine (EDA) as an N element dopant to obtain strong fluorescence N-CQDs possessing a high quantum yield (QY= 18.86 %). Through simple chemical treatments, cellulose was extracted from peanut shells and used as a substrate material for the further preparing bio-based solid-state fluorescence composites named as N-CQDs/cellulose (NKC) based on the coupling reaction of γ-(2,3-epoxypropoxy) propytrimethoxysilane (KH-560). Owing to vast hydroxy groups on cellulose, excellent fluorescence detection performance for Fe3+ of N-CQDs, and the steady coupling and effective dispersion of N-CQDs on cellulose, the obtained NKC boasts not only wonderful stability in water but also highly sensitive assay ability for Fe3+ with a wide range (0–3300 µM) and a low LOD (10.56 µM). Upon being treated with L-cysteine, the NKC demonstrated a potential for recycling, as evidenced by its fluorescence intensity being restored to 93 % in comparison to the original NKC solution.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of Fe3+ detection capable N-CQDs/Cellulose solid-state fluorescence composites.
(a) TEM image (insert: high-resolution image), (b) size distribution and, (c) XRD of N-CQDs; (d) SEM image and (e-h) elemental mapping images of NKC.
(a) XPS spectra of NKC; (b-e) high-resolution spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, Si 2p, and N 1 s of NKC; (f) FTIR spectra of peanut shell, KC, NKC, and N-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and optimal fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs solution, inset: images under daylight (I) and UV (365 nm) (Ⅱ); (b) UV–vis spectra of NKC, KC and N-CODs, inset: images of peanut shell cellulose, NKC under daylight and UV (365 nm), and NKC in water under UV; (c) Excitation-dependent spectra of N-CQDs and (d) NKC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NKC and KC under 360 nm and 390 nm, and the centrifuged supernatant obtained by soaking NKC in deionized water for one week; (b) The impact of storage length on fluorescence intensity of NKC. (c) The optical response (PL emission intensity) of N-CQDs and (d) NKC under various pH values.

(a) Sensitivity studies of NKC to different metal ions (4 mM, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of blank control and add ions); (b) Photoluminescence spectrum of NKC with Fe3+ (0–3300 μM) and (c) corresponding Stern-Volmer calibration curve, inset: fluorescence quenching of NKC by different concentrations of Fe3+; (d) Effects of various metal ions (3300 µM) on NKC+Fe3+ detection system.

(a) UV–vis analysis of N-CQDs solution, Fe3+ solution, and the mixture of both, inset: picture of N-CQDs, Fe3+, and a mixture of both dissolved by water; (b) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs alone and adding Fe3+; (c) Recycled performance testing of NKC.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most reported solid-state fluorescence materials utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) rely on simply physical dispersing in polymer or gel matrices to suppress self-quenching, resulting in the issue of fluorescence instability. Here, we initially utilize crop waste peanut shells and add ethylenediamine (EDA) as an N element dopant to obtain strong fluorescence N-CQDs possessing a high quantum yield (QY= 18.86 %). Through simple chemical treatments, cellulose was extracted from peanut shells and used as a substrate material for the further preparing bio-based solid-state fluorescence composites named as N-CQDs/cellulose (NKC) based on the coupling reaction of γ-(2,3-epoxypropoxy) propytrimethoxysilane (KH-560). Owing to vast hydroxy groups on cellulose, excellent fluorescence detection performance for Fe3+ of N-CQDs, and the steady coupling and effective dispersion of N-CQDs on cellulose, the obtained NKC boasts not only wonderful stability in water but also highly sensitive assay ability for Fe3+ with a wide range (0–3300 µM) and a low LOD (10.56 µM). Upon being treated with L-cysteine, the NKC demonstrated a potential for recycling, as evidenced by its fluorescence intensity being restored to 93 % in comparison to the original NKC solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron performs crucial functions in oxygen transport, electron transfer, and catalytic oxidoreductase reactions (Shen et al., 2017). Nevertheless, excess Fe3+ will also lead to damage to human health (Nagaraj et al., 2022). Therefore, assessing the concentration of Fe3+ is an important factor in evaluating living environmental quality (Gao et al., 2021), and many methods for sensitive detecting Fe3+ have been established such as electrochemical sensors (Pang et al., 2019), ICP-AES (Ramesh et al., 2007), and ICP-MS (Alomar et al., 2021). However, these traditional Fe3+ detection methods are unable to meet the requirements due to complex operation, long pretreatment time, strict requirements for detection conditions, and high cost (Qi et al., 2018; Li et al., 2022).

Recently, CQDs fluorescent probes, based on measuring variations in fluorescence intensity upon interaction with the target analyte have gained a growing interest due to their advantages, including simple preparation processes, exceptional sensitivity in detection, good biocompatibility, and easy of use (Batool et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a,b). Compared to the use of artificial materials, utilizing natural resources to prepare carbon quantum dots is more affordable, abundant, and environmentally friendly, which can enhance the economic value of biomass materials (Wang et al., 2019). Widespread reports about preparing CQDs from biomass and applying in fluorescence probes for Fe3+ detection have been substantiated. For instance, Wang et al. fabricated CQDs from multiple plant materials for sensitive detection of Fe3+ (Wang et al., 2020). Won et al. designed CQDs based on coffee waste for detecting Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (Won and Kim, 2022). However, these Fe3+ fluorescence probes reported are almost all based on liquid CQDs. The dispersion stability of liquid CQDs will decrease with the extension of time (Zhao et al., 2022), thus influencing the stability of detection. Additionally, liquid CQDs are commonly hard to reuse during application.

Recently, due to their good conveniences, wonderful optical properties, and practical performance, the synthesis of solid-state fluorescent materials based on CQDs has received wide attention in fingerprint identification (Wang et al., 2018), fluorescence ink (Jiang et al., 2018), LEDs (Sun et al., 2018), etc. Unfortunately, CQDs usually undergo self-quenching due to aggregation when in the solid state (Zheng et al., 2017; He et al., 2017). It is typically addressed by solidifying CQDs within solid matrices (Xu et al., 2020) to increase the distance of CQDs units and achieve excellent photoluminescence. As an illustration, Lv et al. synthesized CQDs hydrogel by carboxymethyl cellulose/polyacrylamide composite (Lv et al., 2022). El-Shamy et al. combined CQDs and PVA to achieve CQDs films (El-Shamy and Zayied, 2020). These methods primarily utilize polymers or gel matrices as dispersants and mainly rely on physical strategies to prevent direct contact of CQDs, thus resulting in the insufficient stability fluorescence intensity of the obtained CQDs/solid-state photoluminescent composites.

Cellulose, as a hydroxyl-rich biopolymer, has tremendous potential in the field of new functional materials owing to its excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-toxicity, and low cost characteristics (Klemm et al., 2004). The peanut shells are common crop waste rich in cellulose (40 %−65 %) and lignin (26 %−36 %) (Sathiparan et al., 2023) which can be used as an economical source of cellulose and an excellent source of CQDs. Additionally, due to the low N element content in peanut shells, the addition of N element dopants can further enhance the optical properties of peanut shell-derived CQDs (Wang et al., 2021a,b; Xu et al., 2021). Nowadays, it has been reported that γ-(2,3-epoxypropoxy) propytrimethoxysilane (KH-560) is a non-toxic coupling agent containing an epoxy group and three -OCH3 (Ghorbani et al., 2020). The epoxy group on KH-560 is prone to ring-opening reaction with -NH3 (Yang et al., 2021) while the -OCH3 are susceptible to hydrolysis reaction (Xie et al., 2010). These reactions can be utilized to improve the mechanical properties, transparency, thermostabilities, and swelling degree of composites by establishing strong chemical bonds (Chen et al., 2021; Connell et al., 2014; Silva et al., 2017).

Herein, by selecting peanut shells as raw materials, we prepare solid-state fluorescence composites N-CQDs/Cellulose (NKC) for the detection of Fe3+. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the hydrothermal reaction of peanut shell powder and EDA yields N-CQDs with strong QY. Through the coupling effect, the -OH on cellulose and the -NH3 present in N-CQDs undergo reactions with the -OCH3 and epoxy groups of KH-560, respectively. These reactions lead to the steady coupling and effective suppressing self-quenching of N-CQDs on the cellulose. Utilizing the obtained NKC as a solid-state fluorescent probe enables the detection of Fe3+, which not only displays admirable stability, high selectivity, wide detection range, and high sensitivity but also possesses excellent potential for recycling.

Peanut shells were collected by a local food market (Ya'an, China). KH-560 (97%) was acquired from Macklin (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6 H2O, BaCl2·2 H2O, MgCl2·6 H2O, AlCl3·6 H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CuSO4·5 H2O, CoCl2·6 H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, KCl, CrCl3, NaOH, H3BO3, H3PO4, CH3COOH, L-cysteine, and ethylenediamine (EDA) were all analytically pure obtained from Chron corp (AR, Chengdu, China). In all routine experiments, deionized water was utilized, and using the BR (Britton-Robinson) buffer solution (prepared from H3BO3, H3PO4, and CH3COOH, adjusting pH with NaOH) for adjusting pH.

The carbon source utilized was waste peanut shells, and N-CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal process by adding the N element dopant EDA. Typically, the freshly purchased peanut shells were dried at 60 °C for 48 h, then ground into powder using a grinder and sifted through a sieve. Then, a 50 mL volume of deionized water was added to 1.75 g of peanut shell powder, followed by dispersing 0.25 g of EDA into the mixture and heating it to 200 °C for 6 h in the polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitation and retain the supernatant. The supernatant was then passed through a 0.22 µm membrane and subjected to dialysis using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off value of 1000 Da) for 24 h for further purification. The solid N-CQDs were obtained by freeze-drying the aforementioned sample for 48 h, and the resulting powder was dissolved in deionized water to prepare a solution with a concentration of 2 g/L for subsequent experiments. To achieve optimal luminescent performance, the condition experiments were carried out (Supporting information). In addition, we synthesized PS-CQDs using only peanut shell powder without adding EDA under the same conditions.

The above peanut shell powder (10 g) was treated with 200 mL of 2 wt% potassium hydroxide (80◦C for 4 h) to remove hemicellulose. Then, 200 mL of 5 wt% acidified sodium chlorite (pH4∼5, adjusted by glacial acetic acid, 80◦C for 4 h) was used twice to remove lignin to obtain purified peanut shell cellulose. For the subsequent steps, 10 mL of deionized water, 2 mL of KH-560, and 1 mL of acetic acid (serving as a catalyst) were added to 40 mL of ethanol. The mixture was stirred for 10 min before adding 1 g of cellulose. Subsequently, the stirring was continued for 24 h. The filtered solid was washed with methanol and dried at 70 °C for 12 h to yield KH-560 modified cellulose (KC). Next, 1 g of KC was mixed with a 25 mL volume of N-CQDs and stirred for 24 h, the resulting solid was subjected to washing with water and finally freeze-dried to obtain NKC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance of N-CQDs and NKC for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of N-CQDs and NKC for detecting Fe3+, the N-CQDs solution (100 µL) or NKC powder (10 mg) was mixed with 500 µL of FeCl3·6 H2O, BaCl2·2 H2O, MgCl2·6 H2O, AlCl3·6 H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CuSO4·5 H2O, CoCl2·6 H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, KCl or CrCl3 solution and add BR buffer (pH=7) to the total volume of 3 mL to measured the photoluminescence spectrum and record fluorescence intensity (FL. Intensity). The responsiveness of N-CQDs and NKC toward Fe3+ was investigated by recording the fluorescence intensities at different concentrations of Fe3+ solution. The above experiments were all repeated three times in parallel.

NKC (10 mg) was mixed with 3 mL of BR buffer (pH=7) to obtain the original NKC solution, and the photoluminescence spectrum was recorded. A second 10 mg of NKC was mixed with 500 µL of Fe3+ solution and 2.5 mL of BR buffer, followed by recording the photoluminescence spectrum. After adding L-cysteine (50 mg) for 10 min, filter, wash, and dry the aforementioned mixture to recycle NKC. Subsequently, the photoluminescence spectrum of recycled NKC was once again recorded with 3 mL of BR buffer.

The UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs solution, Fe3+ solution and the mixture of both were measured using an A390 spectrophotometer (Aoyi Instruments Co., Ltd, China). UV–vis spectra of NKC and KC were measured using an UV 3600I plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence assays were conducted using an LS-55 (Perkin-Elmer, USA) spectrophotometer. The crystalline phase of N-CQDs was analyzed applying DX-2700B (Dandong Fangyuan Instrument Co., Ltd, China) X-ray diffractometry (XRD), while the microstructure was characterized using a JEOL-2100F (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM) at various magnifications. The analysis of elemental composition and surface groups were assessed utilizing an XSAM800 (Kratos, Britain) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) separately and a Nicolet Is5 (Themor, USA) Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). The QY and fluorescence lifetimes characterization were measured using a FluoroLog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA, Japan). The structural analysis of NKC was conducted using a JSM-7500F (JEOL, Japan) scanning electron microscope (SEM), and the characterization of surface elements was recorded by combining an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and elemental composition analysis of N-CQDs and NKC
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs units observed by TEM in Fig. 2a are nearly spherical, evenly distributed, and do not reveal any aggregations under optimal reaction conditions (Fig. S1). And the lattice fringe distance (inset of Fig. 2a) is measured to be 0.21 nm, signifying the crystal face (100) of graphene (Liu et al., 2022). By conducting a statistical analysis of nanoparticles, the corresponding particle-size distribution histogram can be obtained (Fig. 2b) (Wang et al., 2019), and the results demonstrated that N-CQDs range in size from 2.1 to 4.5 nm and 3.3 ± 0.25 nm on average. Also, the XRD image in Fig. 2 c demonstrates the N-CQDs possess partial graphitization and small size as evidenced by the broad diffraction peak around 25◦ (Gan et al., 2022). And the presence of nitrogen might be associated with the peak observed at 31° (Ávila et al., 2022). The results presented above indicate the successful synthesis of N-CQDs.

In the SEM image presented in Fig. 2d, the NKC has a uniform strip micrograph structure with a length of approximately 200 µm at an optimum reaction ratio (Fig. S2). During the process of detection, the uniform structure of NKC allows for its even dispersion in water. Furthermore, the elemental mapping images of NKC observed by EDS (Fig. 2e-h) provide the distribution evidence of C, O, Si (from KH-560), and N (from N-CQDs) elements. This suggests that N-CQDs are evenly distributed on the cellulose through the KH-560 coupling reaction.

To further study the elemental composition of N-CQDs and NKC, XPS and FTIR were employed. The results displayed in Fig. S3 indicate that N doping of PS-CQDs was successfully performed by using EDA as a nitrogen source under hydrothermal conditions, significantly improving the content of nitrogen-related composition (from 2.68 % to 5.11 %) and QY (Sun et al., 2022) (from 0.72 % to 18.86 %), and the N-CQDs obtained exhibit an increase in amino groups, providing foundations for the connection of KH-560. Moreover, the XPS survey spectrum of NKC ( Fig. 3a) exhibits the coexistence information of C (59.67 %), O (38.50 %), Si (1.28 %), and N (0.55 %) elements. Specifically, the curve of C 1s (Fig. 3b) is fitted with peaks at 284.0 eV (C-C/CC), 284.9 eV (C-N/C-O/Si-O-C), and 287.6 eV (CO). The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) reveals three discernible peaks at 531.4 eV (CO), 532.8 eV (-OH/C-O/Si-O-C), and 533.9 eV (O-C-O). Besides, a single peak appears at 102.07 eV in Fig. 3d corresponding to Si-O-C further confirming the grafting of KH-560 onto cellulose. Last, the N 1s spectrum is deconvoluted to two peaks as depicted in Fig. 3e, one is for C-N-C bonds at 399.15 eV (Zou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019), and the other is for N-H bonds at 400.65 eV (Tiwari et al., 2023), indicating a C-N bond is formed for linking the N-CQDs with KH-560. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum results of N-CQDs and PS-CQDs (Fig. S4) show the peak strength of the C-N bond at 1461 cm−1 is significantly enhanced (Qi et al., 2018), which should be attributed to the successful nitrogen doping into PS-CQDs by EDA. In Fig. 3f, after chemical treatment of the peanut shell powder, the peak at 1510 cm−1 is no longer present, indicating the successful removal of the lignin (main coloring substance) of peanut shell powder (Gan et al., 2022). Besides, for NKC, peaks at 1588 and 1055 cm−1 are attributed to N-H scissoring and Si-O-Si asymmetric vibrational modes respectively (Ávila et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2017). These peaks increase correspondingly after connecting KH-560 and N-CQDs, pointing to the amino groups on N-CQDs and self-hydrolysis of alkoxy groups on KH-560. All of the results demonstrate that N-CQDs are successfully connected and dispersed on cellulose through the coupling effect of KH-560.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of N-CQDs and NKC
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a more thorough knowledge of the optical properties of NKC, we conducted analyses of the spectral performance. For N-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption signals at 279 nm and near 340 nm are clearly observed in Fig. 4a. The first peak corresponds to the π-π * transition of CC structure while the second peak from n-π * transition of CN and CO groups. (Zhu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b). Additionally, the optimal excitation wavelength for the N-CQDs solution is 390 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelength is at 461 nm, which confirms that the N-CQDs emit blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation. In Fig. 4a's inset, it can be also observed that the solution of N-CQDs appears tan during daylight but emits blue light under UV. Distinctly, NKC shows an obvious new UV–vis peak appearing at 279 nm compared with KC, which should belong to the π-π * of N-CQDs (Fig. 4b). This further indicates that N-CQDs are successfully coupling on cellulose. Moreover, the cellulose exhibits strong fluorescence after anchoring N-CQDs (inset of Fig. 4b). The NKC powder can be observed to have a faint yellow color under visible light while appearing bright cyan when exposed to 365 nm UV light. When placed in water and exposed to UV light, the NKC solution exhibits a bright blue color. The phenomena indicate that the self-quenching of solid-state N-CQDs is effectively inhibited through cellulose dispersion and KH-560 coupling effect.

The emission behavior of N-CQDs and NKC both rely on excitation wavelength (Fig. 4c and d). When the exciting light ranges from 320 to 410 nm, the emission peaks of N-CQDs shift from 444 to 484 nm, and NKC shifts from 426 to 481 nm which inherits the fluorescence-dependent behavior of N-CQDs. The direct energy band gap (E gap) based on the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs is approximately 4.76 eV by employing the Tauc plot (Fig. S5). The extra energy bands present in the N-CQDs with lower band gap energy (<4.76 eV) lead to more fluorescent luminescence processes (Yang et al., 2017), suggesting the possible existence of a surface state emission effect results in fluorescence-dependent behavior (Gao et al., 2021). In addition, when NKC is excited at 390 nm, the emission wavelength is as same as N-CQDs (461 nm), implying that the strong fluorescence of NKC should derive from N-CQDs.

The alteration in the optimal excitation wavelength shifted to 360 nm may be ascribed to two factors: (1) The coupling reaction of KH-560 should cause the change in the N-CQDs surface groups, which affects the luminescence of the fluorescent groups; (2) The weak fluorescence of matrix material KC produces fluorescence superposition effects ( Fig. 5a). Additionally, the supernatant obtained by centrifugation after immersing NKC in deionized water for one week (Fig. 5a) had no obvious fluorescence, which indicates the ability of KH-560 to prevent the N-CQDs drop off. The fluorescence intensity of NKC remains almost unchanged (just a decline of 2.1 %) after 4 months of storage (Fig. 5b), which further reveals the coupling effect of KH-560 firmly disperse and anchor N-CQDs on cellulose.

Additionally, the fluorescence characteristics of N-CQDs and NKC were investigated at various pH values in Fig. 5c and d, respectively. Upon pH value increases, the strength of N-CQDs' fluorescence exhibits an initial rise followed by a subsequent decrease, reaching their peak at pH= 7. This result may be ascribed to the variation of protonation state (Li et al., 2021). However, the NKC solution shows little change as pH values increase from 2 to 7 indicating the fluorescence stability of NKC enhances under acidic conditions. The differences in fluorescence performance between N-CQDs and NKC at various pH values may have resulted from the alteration in the surface amino groups of N-CQDs on cellulose, arising from the reaction between N-CQDs and KH-560.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of NKC as fluorescence probes to detect Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S6, the N-CQDs exhibit excellent selectivity for detecting Fe3+ (65.5% PL quenching) compared with other ions and have a linear regression equation fitted as I0/I= 0.00037[Fe3+]+ 0.99991, the coefficient (R2) was determined to be 0.99622 while the LOD was calculated as 6.37 μM (Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b). Additionally, we further investigate the selectivity of NKC through recording the emission intensities of NKC with various metal ions containing K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+ excited at 360 nm ( Fig. 6a). Among them, the Fe3+ showed the greatest quenching effect on the fluorescence signal (71.0 % quenching). Conversely, the impact on emissions was comparatively low for other metal ions (<50%). To assess the sensitivity of NKC for Fe3+, Fe3+ was added to the system at varying concentrations (0–3300 μM, Fig. 6b). As Fe3+ concentration escalated, the detection signals of NKC exhibited a concomitant decline. The regression equation was fitted to be I0/I= 0.00051[Fe3+] + 0.98901 with the coefficient of R2 = 0.99627 (Fig. 6c), and the LOD is 10.56 μM. By introducing additional intrusive metal ions, the NKC's ability to resist interference is studied and the intensities showed little changes compared to containing Fe3+ alone, indicating its excellent anti-interference capacity (Fig. 6d). Overall, the NKC can serve as a reliable solid-state fluorescent probe for assaying Fe3+. Moreover, compared to other reported CQDs fluorescence probes based on plant materials as precursors for detecting Fe3+, the N-CQDs and NKC have wider linear ranges ( Table 1).

Fig. 7a illustrates that the N-CQDs+Fe3+ system produced a unique absorption signal at 288 nm. This peak is distinct from the characteristic peaks of N-CQDs (279 nm) and Fe3+ (295 nm) and confirms the formation of N-CQDs-Fe3+ complex (Zhang et al., 2022a,b). When the N-CQDs and Fe3+ are mixed and dissolved in water, the deepening of the solution color also verifies the formation of the complex (insert in Fig. 7a). According to existing reports, static quenching occurs attributed to the creation of complexes between CQDs and quenching agents (Shah et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2018). Moreover, Fig. 7b displays the average decay time of the N-CQDs solution as 4.78 ns, showcasing almost no differences compared to the addition of Fe3+ (5.05 ns). Therefore, it can be concluded that electron transfer to Fe3+ is not the quenching mechanism, i.e., it is not dynamic quenching. (Huang et al., 2015) but static fluorescence quenching (Zu et al., 2017).

By adding a stronger coordination agent to reduce the influence of Fe3+ (Wu et al., 2017), we further studied the recycling performance of NKC after detecting Fe3+ in Fig. 7c. After treatment with L-cysteine, the fluorescence intensity was restored to 93% compared to the original NKC solution. This result indicates that the NKC has the potential for recycling and once again proves that fluorescence quenching is due to the product of non-fluorescent complexes.

Overall, peanut shells were utilized to obtain blue fluorescent N-CQDs. The doping of nitrogen effectively improved the QY of N-CQDs (up to 18.86 %) and provided a connection site for KH-560. Importantly, the solid-state bright cyan fluorescence composites (NKC) were further prepared using cellulose from peanut shells with excellent stability based on the coupling effect of KH-560. The NKC was success used to detect Fe3+ with high selectivity, wide linear range, high sensitivity, and not influenced by common metal ions. Combined with the characterization research, it was confirmed that the detection mechanism is static quenching and has the potential for recycling after treatment with L-cysteine. The NKC can be a cheap solid-state fluorescent material for further research.

Maojun Zhao: Methodology. Xianxiang Dai: Supervision, Investigation. Yuanyuan Jiang: Review, Investigation. Ruiwu Yang: Supervision. Jinqiu Liao: Conceptualization, Investigation. Yunsong Zhang: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Supervision, Writing-Reviewing, and Editing. Ying Wang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing, and Editing.
